segments.title: Builth at the River Wye crossing
segments.summary: The opening explains Builth Castle as a critical River Wye crossing between northern and southern Wales, even though little masonry survives.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke starts by making the ruined site legible through geography rather than surviving stonework. Builth sits at an important River Wye crossing and helps connect the northern and southern Welsh theaters. The point is that Edward I's castle was not placed for scenery: it protected loyal local interests and controlled movement through a contested border zone.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Builth Castle, notes that mostly earthworks survive, and identifies the River Wye crossing.
The transcript describes the crossing between northern and southern Wales, local loyalty to Edward, and the need to protect those lands.

segments.title: Marcher and Welsh claims before Edward I
segments.summary: The early-history section follows the older motte-and-bailey through Norman, Marcher, and Welsh ownership claims before its 1260 destruction.
segments.body: Before Edward I treats the site as a new project, Builth already has a complicated frontier history. Dr. Clarke describes an earlier motte-and-bailey with two baileys and another boundary wall, then stresses that fights over the castle could be about inheritance and marriage as much as ethnicity. The passage helps viewers see the Welsh Marches as a zone of overlapping claims rather than a simple map line.

evidence.note: The transcript says Builth had a pre-existing motte-and-bailey castle with two baileys and a further boundary wall.
The transcript describes Norman, Marcher, and Welsh lords fighting, intermarrying, and claiming the castle by inheritance.
The transcript says Llywelyn ap Gruffudd took and destroyed the castle in 1260.

segments.title: Edward I's cheap unfinished rebuild
segments.summary: Edward I's rebuild treats Builth as a blank slate, uses existing earthworks to keep costs low, and stops short once the wider Welsh threat changes.
segments.body: This section explains why Builth did not become one of the showpiece Edwardian castles. The older castle was damaged enough that Edward treated the site as a blank slate, but existing earthworks still made it cheaper than the great northern projects. The transcript ties the unfinished state to money and changing strategic urgency after Llywelyn ap Gruffudd's death, then notes that Builth survived the 1294 revolt and passed to Edward's son.

evidence.note: The transcript says Edward I considered the site a blank slate, began work in May 1277, and left the original castle unfinished in August 1282 for lack of money.
The transcript links the halt to reduced need after Llywelyn ap Gruffudd's death and says the existing earthworks kept costs under 1700 pounds.
The transcript says the castle was besieged but not taken in 1294, granted to Edward's son in 1301, and later held by Roger Mortimer.

segments.title: Mortimer, Isabella, and Edward III
segments.summary: Builth's ownership becomes entangled with Isabella, Roger Mortimer, Edward II's fall, and Edward III's recovery of royal authority.
segments.body: The castle's later story becomes court politics as much as Welsh frontier warfare. Edward II gives Builth to Isabella, who leases it back to Roger Mortimer, creating one of the links in the relationship that later shapes the coup against Edward II. Edward III's adult takeover then reverses Mortimer's power: Mortimer is executed for treason, his properties are seized, and Builth moves into the hands of Gilbert Talbot.

evidence.note: The transcript says Edward II gave the castle to Isabella, who leased it to Roger Mortimer, and that Mortimer became Isabella's lover.
The transcript links Mortimer and Isabella to the coup against Edward II, then describes Edward III's capture of them and Mortimer's execution.
The transcript says Edward III seized Mortimer's properties and Builth was taken by Gilbert Talbot, Justiciar of South Wales.

segments.title: A troubled chain of owners
segments.summary: Builth passes through Alice de Lacy, Hugh de Freyne, the Black Prince, the Mortimers, Owain Glyndwr's war, the Wars of the Roses, and Tudor ruin.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses Builth to show how castle ownership could track personal vulnerability, royal favor, and dynastic upheaval. Alice de Lacy's grant and coerced remarriage lead into later transfers to the Black Prince and a renewed Mortimer rental arrangement with wartime garrison rights. The castle is attacked during Owain Glyndwr's revolt, repaired in 1409, folded back into royal politics during the Wars of the Roses, and eventually loses its masonry after fire and local stone-robbing.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Edward III's grant to Alice de Lacy and Eubulus le Strange, Alice's forced remarriage to Hugh de Freyne, and Freyne becoming lord of Builth.
The transcript describes Alice's death, Edward's grant to the Black Prince, a renewed Mortimer rental arrangement, wartime garrison rights, Glyndwr's attack, and a 1409 repair.
The transcript follows the end of the rental arrangement, Yorkist and Tudor inheritance, the March lordship, Henry VIII's reign, fire, and the disappearance of the stonework.

segments.title: A defensive castle at the edge of control
segments.summary: The closing argument treats Builth as the inland exception that clarifies the Ring of Iron: a river-edge defensive site protecting already controlled land.
segments.body: Builth is useful precisely because it is not an obvious maritime castle. Dr. Clarke calls it a challenge to the sea-power argument, then explains that its position on a large river and at the edge of English-controlled territory makes it more defensive than offensive. The castle was about holding the part of Wales already under control, so investment fell away once that security problem looked less urgent.

evidence.note: The transcript says Builth is far from the sea, a challenge to the maritime-power argument, but still at the edge of a large river and controlled territory.
The transcript says the castle is more defensive than offensive and is about defending the part of Wales already controlled.
The transcript jokes about the castle's unlucky ownership history after explaining its defensive purpose.
